mentions.note: Fine to speculate given ties to former Seattle Genetics leadership, but Cramer stresses this is speculation, not investing, given the cash burn.
Stock was down on the day; Cramer says it's a good level to start buying and to add more if it falls further.
Cramer's preferred gold miner, also more than doubling in 2025 alongside Newmont.
Over 40 million US users, expanding into the UK, and a phenomenal underwriting team; Cramer sees a run to par ($100).
Up 108% in 2025 with tripling revenue and seemingly no direct competitor; expensive at 43x earnings but the growth is real, not purely speculative.
Life-science office REIT down almost 50% in 2025 after slashing its dividend 45% on weak tenant demand; the high yield was masking real problems.
Trades at just 12x this year's earnings estimates with a continuing turnaround; Cramer wants the multi-year move, not the one-day pop.
Down 39% in 2025; a secularly challenged cable business with a stock that's been sinking for three straight years.
Cheapest big bank at 12x earnings, buying back stock aggressively, and positioned to benefit from expected rate cuts via the Discover network.

Trust trimmed some shares during the 2025 slide on re-up/membership softness and CFO Richard Galante's departure, but Larry Williams's valuation, accumulation, and cycle indicators all flag Costco as a 'screaming buy' into 2026.
Down roughly 32% in 2025 on market-share losses; too expensive at 23x earnings to call a bottom yet.
Cramer's preferred enterprise-software pick over Atlassian for a 2026 comeback; had a nice bounce the day of the show.
Venezuela oil trade already priced in at the open; upside from any production increase would be a drop in the bucket for Chevron regardless.
Down 49% in 2025 on tariff fears and a Hoka slowdown; valuation looks reasonable but every dip-buyer has been burned so far.
Second-worst S&P performer, down 67% after an October earnings collapse, guidance cut, and management shakeup.
Cheap at 17x earnings versus the S&P average and a huge beneficiary of 2026's M&A and IPO issuance boom; rallied nearly 4% with more room to run.
Speculative play on eventual Venezuelan oilfield rebuild contracts, drawing an analogy to Iraq; stock opened too high to chase.
Long-term bull on Robinhood's younger, wealth-inheriting user base, but expects near-term trading to keep tracking crypto and speculative names lower.
Up 152% in 2025 on an FDA approval for a rare lung-disease drug, though a disappointing sinusitis trial pulled it back 18% from highs; worth watching, not yet a full recommendation.
Up 84% in 2025 on a turnaround under CEO Lip-Bu Tan, aided by roughly $9B in Trump-administration investment and $5B from Nvidia; headed in the right direction.
Down almost 48% in 2025 as AI erodes demand for its research and advisory services; Cramer isn't willing to call a bottom.
Planned orthopedics spin-off should lift the core pharma multiple, echoing the Kenvue precedent; today's dip is a terrific entry.
Same Venezuela-rebuild speculation as Halliburton, based on the Iraq contracting precedent.
Up 93% in 2025 as another direct beneficiary of memory makers' capacity spending; expect continued big orders.
Cramer calls it a good speculation given its exposure to two of his favorite themes: space and national security.
Has already run a great deal but Cramer still likes it, even as he prefers J&J as the better relative entry right now.
Semicap equipment maker up 137% in 2025 as memory makers keep ordering capacity; jumped over 5% the day of the show.
Grouped with the other Gulf refiners as a Venezuela-crude beneficiary that opened too high to chase.
Down 47.5% in 2025 as a leveraged Bitcoin proxy; Cramer says buy Bitcoin directly instead of the leveraged wrapper.
Longtime show favorite, up 239% in 2025 on AI memory demand; still liked, but Cramer wants holders to take some off the table.
Gold miner up 168% in 2025 on the surge in gold prices, though Cramer prefers Agnico Eagle within the group.
Tough competitive position against larger, better-resourced rivals.
At 42x earnings the multiple is still too high given software's struggle to keep pace with AI-driven hardware demand.
Investing Club members who held through 2025 made huge gains; anyone who recently sold might regret it and should consider buying back.
Up 135% in 2025; the 175x earnings valuation is hard to justify, but Cramer remains a believer and sees no reason to back away.
Same heavy-crude-refiner speculation as Valero; already overinflated by the time of the open.
Down over 31% in 2025 as core payments commoditize and it lags in buy-now-pay-later and stablecoins, but now trades at just 10x earnings — maybe 2026 is when it gets too cheap to ignore.
Oilfield-services name speculatively tied to a Venezuelan rebuild; already overextended by the open.
Best S&P performer of 2025, up 371% on data-center memory demand; take some profits after a run this large.
The post-earnings sell-off looked overdone; Cramer still rates CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy highly and calls the stock a buy.
Disc-drive/storage play up 219% in 2025 on data-center demand; same take-some-profits advice as its memory peers.
Down over 33% in 2025 as AI coding tools reduce demand for collaboration software; not the enterprise-software name to own for a 2026 comeback.
Worst S&P performer of 2025, down about 68%, as programmatic ad-tech loses ground to deep-pocketed competitors like Amazon; still needs to prove it can adapt to AI.
Seen as a beneficiary of Venezuelan heavy crude re-routing, but the stock already popped too far before the open to be actionable.
Netflix's roughly $83B bid and Paramount Skydance's competing offer make further upside likely if a higher bid emerges.
Up 282% in 2025 on the same memory/storage boom as former parent SanDisk; trim into strength.
Former high-flying growth stock now trading for very little; Cramer sees real value and says buy some and put it away.
